title: Garden Planting Calendar 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
h1: What to Plant Each Month in the Northern Hemisphere
description: Discover what to plant each month in the Northern Hemisphere. Free planting calendar for Mexico, USA, Spain & Europe.
intro: A **Garden Planting Calendar** tells you exactly which vegetables, herbs, and flowers to start indoors, direct-sow, or transplant outdoors each month based on your location in the Northern Hemisphere. The core logic relies on each crop's **Days to Last Frost (DLF)** offset: Planting Date = Last Spring Frost Date − (Days to Maturity + Indoor Start Lead Time). Because the Northern Hemisphere experiences spring from March–May and fall from September–November, cool-season crops (lettuce, kale, peas) thrive in March–April and August–September, while warm-season crops (tomatoes, peppers, corn, squash) require soil temperatures above 60 °F and go in from May–June. Use this calendar whenever you're planning a new growing season, succession-planting for continuous harvests, or timing a fall garden.
keyTakeaway: **Planting Window = Last Frost Date − Crop Lead Time (weeks).** For most of the Northern Hemisphere (USDA Zones 5–7), the last spring frost falls between April 1–May 15; tomatoes go in 2 weeks after that date, while cool-season crops like peas can tolerate soil as cold as 40 °F and are sown 4–6 weeks *before* last frost.
useCases: A home gardener in Chicago (Zone 6a, last frost ~May 1) wants to know when to start tomato seedlings indoors — answer: 6–8 weeks before May 1 = mid-March.
A backyard farmer in Seattle (Zone 8b, last frost ~March 15) needs to schedule successions of lettuce every 2 weeks from February through April to avoid bolting in summer heat.

A community garden coordinator in Dallas (Zone 8a) is planning a fall garden and needs to know the latest date to direct-sow winter squash before first autumn frost (~November 15) — counting 90 days back = mid-August.
A beginner gardener wants a month-by-month checklist of what to plant, transplant, and harvest without needing to calculate frost dates manually.
explanation:
## How It's Calculated

The calendar uses two core formulas based on USDA Hardiness Zone data and individual crop requirements:

```
Earliest Outdoor Planting Date = Last Spring Frost Date − Frost Tolerance Buffer
Latest Fall Planting Date      = First Fall Frost Date − Days to Maturity
Indoor Start Date              = Earliest Outdoor Date − Indoor Lead Time (weeks)
```

**Example — Tomatoes in Zone 6 (last frost May 1):**
```
Indoor Start  = May 1 − 8 weeks  = ~March 6
Transplant    = May 1 + 2 weeks  = ~May 15  (soil ≥ 60 °F)
Days Outside  = ~120 days growing season
```

**Example — Peas in Zone 6:**
```
Direct Sow   = May 1 − 6 weeks  = ~March 20  (peas tolerate 28 °F)
Harvest      = March 20 + 60 days = ~May 19
```

---

## Reference Table — Monthly Planting Guide (Northern Hemisphere, Zones 5–8)

| Month | Season Phase | Start Indoors | Direct Sow / Transplant Outdoors | Soil Temp Target |
|-------|-------------|---------------|----------------------------------|-----------------|
| **January** | Deep winter | Onions, leeks, celery (8–10 wks ahead) | — | N/A |
| **February** | Late winter | Peppers, eggplant (10–12 wks); broccoli | Cold frames: spinach, mâche | 35–40 °F |
| **March** | Early spring | Tomatoes, basil (6–8 wks) | Peas, radishes, lettuce, kale | 40–50 °F |
| **April** | Spring | Cucumbers, squash, melons (3–4 wks) | Beets, carrots, Swiss chard, spinach | 50–60 °F |
| **May** | Late spring | — | Tomatoes, peppers, beans, corn, squash | 60–70 °F |
| **June** | Early summer | Fall broccoli, cabbage (for Aug transplant) | Sweet potatoes, basil, more beans | 65–75 °F |
| **July** | Summer | Kale, collards (for fall) | Beans (succession), cucumbers | 70–80 °F |
| **August** | Late summer | — | Fall broccoli, kale, turnips, beets, carrots | 65–75 °F |
| **September** | Early fall | — | Spinach, garlic (cloves), lettuce, radishes | 50–60 °F |
| **October** | Fall | — | Garlic (main planting), cover crops (rye, clover) | 40–55 °F |
| **November** | Late fall | — | Overwintering onion sets (Zones 7+) | 35–45 °F |
| **December** | Winter | — | Paperwhites, cold-stratify seeds | N/A |

---

## Typical Cases with Real Numbers

### Case 1 — April, Chicago (Zone 6a)
A gardener selects **April** and lists *tomato, bell pepper, corn*. The tool returns:
- **Tomatoes & Peppers**: Still in the indoor phase. April 1 = ~4 weeks before last frost (May 1). Keep under grow lights; do NOT transplant yet. Harden off starting mid-April (set outside 1 hr/day, increasing over 7–10 days).
- **Corn**: Cannot be direct-sown until soil reaches 60 °F (~May 15 in Zone 6). April selection returns a warning: *"Too early for corn outdoors — wait until May."*
- **What to direct-sow in April instead**: peas, lettuce, radishes, spinach, beets (all tolerate light frost).

### Case 2 — September, Atlanta (Zone 7b, first fall frost ~November 20)
Days until frost: ~80 days.
- **Broccoli transplants**: 80 days to maturity → borderline; start from transplant, not seed.
- **Kale**: 55–60 days → ✅ direct sow September 1–15.
- **Spinach**: 40–50 days → ✅ direct sow through late September.
- **Garlic**: Plant October 1–15 for summer 2026 harvest.

### Case 3 — June, Pacific Northwest (Zone 8b, Seattle)
Summer arrives late; soil hits 60 °F by late May.
- **Beans & corn**: Direct sow June 1–15 (last chance for full maturity before September rains).
- **Winter squash**: Sow June 1 for 95-day variety — harvests ~September 4. ✅
- **Fall brassicas**: Start broccoli and cabbage indoors June 15 for August transplant.

---

## Common Mistakes

1. **Planting warm-season crops too early.** Tomatoes set out when soil is below 55 °F suffer chilling injury, stop growing, and become more susceptible to disease — even if no frost occurs. Always check soil temp, not just air temp.

2. **Ignoring "days to maturity" for fall planting.** A 100-day butternut squash planted August 15 in Zone 6 (first frost October 1) only gets 47 days — it will not mature. Count backwards from first fall frost using the crop's DTM.

3. **Confusing "last frost date" with a guarantee.** USDA/NOAA frost dates represent the 50th percentile historical probability. There is still a 50% chance of frost after that date. Conservative gardeners use the 10% probability date, which can be 2–4 weeks later.

4. **Skipping hardening off.** Seedlings grown indoors under artificial light will sunscald and wilt if transplanted directly outdoors. A 7–14 day hardening-off period (gradual outdoor exposure) is required for all transplants.

5. **Succession planting neglect.** Direct-sowing all lettuce at once leads to a feast-and-famine cycle. Sow a new row every 14 days from March through May for continuous harvest through June.

6. **Wrong planting depth.** Carrot seeds sown deeper than ¼ inch germinate poorly; onion sets planted deeper than 1 inch may not bulb correctly. Each crop has a specific depth requirement (USDA/Extension guidelines).

---

## Related Calculators

- Explore [Days to Maturity Calculator](/days-to-maturity) to find out exactly when your crops will be ready to harvest.
- Use the [Last Frost Date Lookup](/last-frost-date) to find your specific frost dates by ZIP code.
- Try the [Succession Planting Scheduler](/succession-planting) to stagger sowing dates for continuous yields.
- Check the [Soil Temperature Guide](/soil-temperature-guide) to match soil conditions to crop requirements.

faq.q: What can I plant in April in the Northern Hemisphere?
faq.a: April is ideal for direct-sowing cool-season crops: peas, lettuce, spinach, radishes, beets, Swiss chard, and carrots (in Zones 5–8). Soil should be at least 40–50 °F. Simultaneously, warm-season seedlings like tomatoes and peppers should still be growing indoors under lights and will be transplanted after last frost, typically in May.

faq.q: When is the last frost date and why does it matter so much?
faq.a: The last spring frost date is the historical date after which freezing temperatures (≤32 °F) are unlikely — based on 30-year NOAA climate normals. It determines when warm-season crops can safely go outside and when to count backwards for indoor seed-starting. For example, Zone 6 averages a last frost of May 1; Zone 8 averages March 15. USDA and NOAA publish these by location.

faq.q: Can I plant tomatoes in May everywhere in the Northern Hemisphere?
faq.a: Not everywhere. May transplanting is appropriate for USDA Zones 5–7 (much of the continental US). In Zone 4 (Minnesota, northern New England), last frost can extend into late May, pushing transplant dates to early June. In Zone 9–10 (Southern California, South Florida), tomatoes are planted in February–March and again in August–September to avoid summer heat above 95 °F, which prevents fruit set.

faq.q: What is the difference between 'start indoors' and 'direct sow'?
faq.a: 'Start indoors' means germinating seeds in trays or pots inside (under grow lights or near a sunny window) weeks before outdoor planting — used for slow-growing crops like tomatoes (6–8 weeks), peppers (10–12 weeks), and celery (10–12 weeks). 'Direct sow' means placing seeds directly into garden soil — used for crops that dislike transplanting (carrots, beets, corn, beans, peas) or that grow so fast indoor starts aren't necessary (radishes, 25–30 days to maturity).

faq.q: What is 'hardening off' and how long does it take?
faq.a: Hardening off is the gradual acclimatization of indoor-grown seedlings to outdoor conditions — sunlight intensity, wind, and temperature fluctuation. Start by placing seedlings outside in a sheltered, partially shaded spot for 1–2 hours on Day 1, increasing exposure by 1–2 hours per day over 7–14 days. Skipping this step causes sunscald (white/bleached leaves) or transplant shock, which can set plants back 2–3 weeks.

faq.q: How do I plan a fall garden — when do I start planting in late summer?
faq.a: Count backwards from your first fall frost date using each crop's 'Days to Maturity' (DTM). Formula: Latest Sow Date = First Fall Frost − DTM − 14 days (for slower fall growth due to cooling temperatures). Example: First frost October 15, broccoli DTM = 80 days → plant by July 27. Fast crops like radishes (30 days) or spinach (40–50 days) can go in as late as mid-September in Zone 6.

faq.q: Which crops can survive a light frost and which cannot?
faq.a: Hardy crops (survive 25–28 °F): kale, collards, Brussels sprouts, parsnips, garlic, leeks. Semi-hardy crops (survive 29–32 °F): broccoli, cabbage, carrots, lettuce, peas, spinach, beets. Tender crops (damaged below 32 °F): tomatoes, peppers, cucumbers, beans, basil, corn, squash, melons. This distinction, documented by USDA Cooperative Extension services, determines which crops can be planted earliest in spring and latest in fall.

faq.q: How does USDA Plant Hardiness Zone affect what I plant and when?
faq.a: USDA Plant Hardiness Zones (updated 2023, based on 1991–2020 climate normals) are divided into 13 zones based on average annual minimum winter temperatures, in 10 °F increments. Zone 5 (−20 to −10 °F) has a short growing season (~150 frost-free days); Zone 9 (20–30 °F) has ~270+ frost-free days. The zone directly determines last/first frost dates, which are the anchor points for all planting calculations. Find your zone at the USDA Plant Hardiness Zone Map (planthardiness.ars.usda.gov).
